segments.s738-g25: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of'. The nouns کرسی، خرگاه، پرده سرای are listed with و linking them; پرده modifies سرای to mean a curtained room or curtained part of the house (a compound idea). The list is cumulative: items taken 'from' those places.
segments.s738-g26: همان is the demonstrative 'that/the very', placed before خیمه to single it out. و connects to the next phrase; آخر is an adverb meaning 'last/finally' (here 'at last'), modifying چارپای 'four-footed animal' which in context means pack-animals or beasts of burden.
segments.s738-g27: The copula بود (past) follows the noun phrase in this poetic order: شتر بود پیش اندرون پانصد = 'there were camels, five hundred, before the inner quarters.' پیش اندرون is a locative phrase 'in front of/at the entrance to the inner quarters.' پانصد follows for numeric emphasis.
segments.s738-g28: همه 'all' is the subject/agent; کرده is the perfect participle 'having made' (used here with active sense 'had made'); آن بزم را is the definite object (marked by را) 'that feast'; نامزد is used as a predicate or object-complement meaning 'a betrothal/engagement' — so literally 'they had all made that feast an engagement.'
segments.s738-g29: ز here means 'from' or 'among'; شاهان specifies origin/association 'of the kings'. برنای is an adjective 'youthful/young' modifying سیصد سوار 'three hundred riders' — poetic word order places elements for rhythm and emphasis.
segments.s738-g30: همی‌راند is the classical continuous/iterative past form (همی- + راند) meaning 'was riding/rode on' or 'kept riding'; با introduces the companion 'with'; نامور شهریار = 'the renowned/famed sovereign' with the adjective preceding the noun.
segments.s738-g31: This is a list of garments and ornaments: the first two words form items followed by two more items joined with و (and). Word order is item1, و, item2, و, adjective + noun (زرین کمر = 'golden belt'). Note that classical Persian often strings nouns and adjectives like this without an explicit verb; the verb appears in the next segment.
segments.s738-g32: بهر here means 'for' or 'in order to' and introduces the purpose/placement; مهره‌ای is an indefinite noun ('a bead' or 'an amulet') with the indefinite -ای, در نشانده is a past participle phrase 'has placed / set in', where نشانده ('placed') is the verb form that links back to the implied actor from the preceding line, and گهر is the direct object 'a jewel'. The phrase literally reads 'for a bead, in (it) he placed a jewel'.
segments.s738-g81: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause ('when'); the prepositional phrases از کوه و ز دشت ('from the mountain and from the plain') give sources, and برداشت is a past third-person singular of برداشتن meaning 'took/raised/left' (here 'left' or 'set out'). وز here is a poetic contraction of و + ز ('and from') linking the two sources. بهر marks direction/purpose 'toward'. Word order places the source phrases before the verb, as common in poetry.
segments.s738-g82: همی‌رفت is an imperfective/past-continuous poetic form meaning 'was going' or 'went on'; شادی کنان is a noun plus the participial/gerundial کنان ('doing') forming an adverbial phrase 'rejoicing' or 'in joy'; سوی شهر is the directional phrase 'toward the city'. The adverbial 'شادی کنان' modifies the verb 'همی‌رفت'.
segments.s738-g83: ببستند is a past plural verb meaning 'they tied/strung/fastened' (here 'they hung/strung up'); آذین is the direct object 'decorations'; بشهر and به راه are locative/directional phrases ('in the city' and 'on/along the road') describing where the decorations were arranged. The verb precedes its object and locatives in this poetic ordering.
segments.s738-g84: که introduces an explanatory/subordinate clause ('for/that'), here giving the reason: 'that the king came'. شاه آمد is a simple past clause 'the king came'; از دشت نخچیرگاه marks the source 'from the plain (of the) hunting-ground'. Word order is subject + past verb + source phrase.
segments.s738-g85: ز is the poetic ablative/causal 'from' or 'because of'; نالیدن is a verbal noun here meaning 'the sounding/wailing'; بوق و بانگ are coordinated instruments/sounds ('trumpet and cry'); سرود is a nominative noun 'song' produced by those sounds. The whole phrase expresses source/cause: 'from the trumpets' sounding and cries, (came) song.'
segments.s738-g86: هوا گشت is a nominal + past of گشتن meaning 'the air became' or 'the atmosphere turned'; ز introduces the cause/source again ('from the voice/sound'); آواز here means 'voice/sound'; بی‌تار و پود is a poetic phrase literally 'without string (tar) and weft'—i.e. without woven support, a metaphor meaning unstrung or purely vocal. The clause says the air was transformed into pure voice/music, unbound by strings or fabric.
segments.s738-g87: چنان is a demonstrative/adverb meaning 'so/thus' or 'such'; خسروی is an adjective/noun 'regal, kingly' describing the figure; برز is an adjective 'high/exalted' and is coordinated with شاخ بلند ('tall antlers/branch'); the sequence is poetic apposition, with descriptive adjectives placed after the noun.
segments.s738-g88: ز دشت اندر آمد is a clause 'came out of/from the plain' where ز is ablative 'from', اندر آمد is a classical/poetic verb phrase 'entered/came in'; به کاخ بلند gives the destination 'to the lofty palace'. Word order shows source phrase before the verb and destination after it, typical of elevated poetic diction.
segments.s738-g33: دوصد برده is the subject phrase (numeral + noun): Persian places the numeral before the counted noun. تامجمر (تا + مجمر fused in poetry) names the censer/brazier that they set alight and functions as the object/goal of the action. افروختند is 3rd person plural past (perfective) of افروز — “they kindled/lit” — placed at the end as the finite verb, completing the clause.
segments.s738-g34: This clause states what burned on/at the brazier. برو here is an archaic/poetic fused form meaning essentially “on it / upon that” (بر او) rather than the common verb “go”; it marks location. عود و عنبر are coordinated nouns joined by و, and همی‌سوختند is the imperfective/past-continuous 3rd person plural (همی‑ prefix indicates continuous/habitual action in classical style) — “they were burning.”
segments.s738-g35: دوصد مرد is the head noun phrase (numeral + noun). برنای and فرمانبران are adjectival modifiers placed after the noun in the classical register: برنای = “youthful/young” and فرمانبران = “obedient/submissive” (plural adjective/epithet modifying مرد). Word order here is typical Persian: noun then post-positive modifiers.
segments.s738-g36: ابا is here a noun (a garment, “aba/cloak”) used as the possession/accoutrement; the listed senses did not include this noun, so its sense is marked -1. هریکی is a distributive pronoun ‘each one’ that specifies distribution of the following items. نرگس و زعفران are coordinated nouns (narcissus and saffron) describing what each man carried/wore; the whole phrase means “each one (had) an aba, a narcissus and saffron.”
segments.s738-g37: همه پیش بردند: همه (everybody/all) is the subject; پیش here functions adverbially as ‘forward / in front’ or ‘toward’ and combines with بردند (3rd pl. past of بردن, “to carry/take”) — standard past-tense verb-final order. تا introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that / in order that'), and باد بوی is a genitival/attributive grouping (بادِ بوی) meaning ‘the wind’s scent’ or more literally ‘wind/scent’ depending on reading.
segments.s738-g38: چو آید is a temporal subordinate clause: چو = 'when' and آید is 3rd sg. present/subjunctive of آمدن 'to come' (classical usage often uses present/subjunctive after چو). ز هر سو is a prepositional phrase 'from every side' (ز = poetic ‘from’, هر سو = every direction). رساند is a 3rd person singular form meaning 'deliver/convey' used here in the result/purpose sense governed by تا. بدوی is an enclitic/directional form (بدو + ی) meaning roughly 'to him' in this context; this specific use is not among the provided senses so it is marked -1.
segments.s738-g39: همه پیش آنکس is 'they all before that person' — همه (all) + پیش (before/in front of) + آنکس (that person). The relative/subordinate clause is introduced by که (who/that). با بوی خوش is an instrumental/attendant phrase 'with a pleasant scent' modifying آنکس; بوی modifies خوش (pleasant scent).
segments.s738-g40: همی‌رفت is the imperfective/past continuous 3rd person singular (همی‑ prefix plus رفتن) — 'was going / walked'. با + مشک marks accompaniment or instrument 'with musk'. صد (a numeral, present in the line though not listed as a word item) modifies آبکش: 'a hundred آبکش' (i.e. a hundred small vessels/scoops). آبکش here names the vessels used with water/perfume; the exact everyday sense is contextual (sprinkling- or water‑vessels) rather than the literal kitchen sieve sense from the dictionary entry, but morpho‑syntactically it is the counted noun governed by صد.
segments.s738-g65: This is a rhetorical nominal phrase introduced by the exclamatory/ interrogative کجا ‘where’. آن همه is a fixed idiomatic quantifier meaning ‘all those / so many’. مهر و خونین سرشک coordinates two nouns: مهر ‘love/affection’ comes first, then خونین سرشک ‘blood-red teardrop’ (خونین modifies سرشک). The noun order and quantifier are typical of Persian poetic inversion.
segments.s738-g66: که introduces a relative clause modifying the preceding phrase (‘that…’). دیدار here functions as ‘the sight/meeting (of)’ (classical use of دیدار for seeing/meeting). شیرین is the proper name Shirin. بد precedes and qualifies پزشک in a somewhat inverted/poetic order meaning ‘a bad / harmful physician’; او را + را marks the person affected (him) as the object of that bad ‘physician’ effect. Grammatically the clause says roughly ‘that the sight of Shirin was a bad physician to him.’
segments.s738-g67: Another rhetorical opening: کجا آن همه ‘where are all those…’. روز کردن به شب is a verbal phrase meaning ‘to turn day into night’ (کردن = ‘to make / to turn’). The preposition به here has the sense ‘into/towards’ and governs شَب ‘night’; the light verb کردن follows the noun it affects, a common Persian word order.
segments.s738-g68: This coordinates bodily images: دل و دیده ‘heart and eye’ followed by the paired participial adjectives گریان و خندان ‘weeping and smiling’. In Persian poetry adjectives/participles can precede or follow their nouns; here گریان modifies دل و دیده, and خندان applies to دو لب (two lips) which follows, giving the sense ‘heart and eye weeping, and the two lips smiling.’ The order stresses the contrast between inner sorrow and outer smile.
segments.s738-g69: Again starting with the rhetorical کجا آن همه. بند و پیوندما coordinates two synonyms meaning ‘ties/bonds’; بند is the noun ‘bond/fetter’ and پیوندما is a possessive compound (پیوند + ما) meaning ‘our bond/union’ — the suffixal or enclitic مَا marks possession in this poetic concatenation.
segments.s738-g70: The same interrogative pattern repeats. عهد و سوگند ما lists two close words for pledged promises: عهد ‘oath/pledge’ and سوگند ‘vow/oath’, with ما ‘we/us’ appended to mark them as ours. The repetition underscores the speaker’s lament about broken promises.
segments.s738-g71: همی‌گفت is an imperfect/habitual past form meaning ‘he kept saying / he would say’ (the prefixed همی- marks continuous/habitual aspect in classical Persian). وز here is an archaic preposition (= از) meaning ‘from’ (this sense is not listed in the provided senses). دیده is ‘eye,’ and خوناب ‘blood‑water / bloody discharge’ is the noun; زرد modifies خوناب as ‘yellow/pale.’ The phrase means ‘from his eyes (there) poured yellow blood‑water.’
segments.s738-g72: همی‌ریخت is the habitual/imperfect past of ریختن ‘to pour/flow/scatter’, so ‘was pouring’ or ‘kept pouring’. برجامه is the prepositional phrase بر + جامه ‘upon the garment/robe’. لاژورد names the robe’s color/material (lapis‑lazuli/azure). Word order places the verb first for poetic effect, then the prepositional destination.
segments.s738-g9: هزار و چهل is the cardinal number 1,040 (هزار ‘thousand’ linked with چهل ‘forty’ by و). The coordinated noun phrase چوب و شمشیر lists two items (staves/wooden staffs and swords). داشت is simple past third-person singular indicating possession (‘he had’ or ‘he carried’); the verb follows the noun phrase in this poetic order.
segments.s738-g10: که is the relative/introductory particle ‘that/who’ introducing a clause linked to the previous possession. دیبای is the brocade (a fine silk garment) that is being described. در بر ... داشت is a poetic phrasing equivalent to ‘to have on/wear’ (در بر داشتن = to wear), and زره زیر is an inverted order meaning ‘under the armor’ (زیر normally precedes the noun but is placed after for style/meter). داشت repeats the past possession verb, tying this clause back to the subject.
segments.s738-g11: پس اندر is a temporal/location frame ‘then / within’. بدی is an archaic or obscure term here (likely indicating a retinue, camp, or inner place) and is semantically uncertain in modern Persian; پانصد gives the number 500, and بازدار (used collectively) means ‘restrainer/warden/keeper’ — a nominal group indicating five hundred guards. The clause is nominal and elliptical (the verb ‘had’ is understood from context).
segments.s738-g12: هم از ... کار attaches qualifications to those wardens: هم ‘also’, از here introduces the domain/skill ('in/from'), followed by a list of things they practise. واشه is an obscure/uncertain term in this context (marked as unclear). چرغ is ‘lamp / light’ (here likely meaning work/skills involving lamps or signals), and شاهین کار is a compound meaning ‘falcon-work’ — i.e. falconry or being a falconer; کار functions as ‘work/skill’. The whole phrase modifies ‘بازدار’ from the previous segment.
segments.s738-g13: ازان پس is an archaic/poetic temporal phrase ‘after that / thereupon’. برفتند is past tense third-person plural ‘they went / rode off’. سیصد سوار is the noun phrase ‘three hundred riders/horsemen’; note the verb precedes the noun phrase here, a common poetic order.
segments.s738-g14: پس resumes the temporal sequence (‘then’). بازداران is the plural of بازدار (‘wardens/keepers’); با is the preposition ‘with’; یوزدار is a compound meaning ‘cheetah-keeper’ or huntsman who handles the yūz (cheetah). The verb is omitted (elliptical) and understood from the previous clause.
segments.s738-g15: به زنجیر is a prepositional phrase ‘with/by chain’ meaning ‘in chains’ or ‘chained’. هفتاد gives the number seventy. شیروپلنگ is a compound noun ‘lion-and-panther’ (large predatory felines, here meaning lions and leopards/panthers). The segment introduces what is bound; the main adjectival verb follows.
segments.s738-g16: به دیبای چین indicates location/setting: ‘into/within folds (چین) of brocade (دیبا)’. اندرون ‘inside’ reinforces that they are within the interior; بسته is a past-participial adjective ‘bound/tied’ describing the animals, and تنگ ‘tight’ (adverbially/adjectivally) modifies بسته. Word order is inverted for poetic effect, but the sense is ‘(were) tightly bound inside the brocade folds.’
segments.s738-g41: 'که' here is the conjunction/relative 'that' linking this clause to what precedes; 'تا' is the temporal 'until' introducing the event; 'ناورد' is an older past verb meaning 'assailed/attacked' (subject–verb inversion is poetic: verb appears before its subject); 'ناگهان' is the adverb 'suddenly'; 'گرد باد' is a compound 'dust‑wind' = 'whirlwind' (گرد = dust, باد = wind). The clause literally presents 'until assailed suddenly (was) the whirlwind'.
segments.s738-g42: 'نشاند' is an archaic/perfective verb meaning 'set/placed' or 'caused to sit' and here functions as 'fell upon/settled on'; 'بران' = 'upon that' pointing to the previously mentioned person; 'شاه فرخ نژاد' is a titulary noun phrase 'the king, fortunate‑born' (فرخ‑نژاد is an epithet). The verb governs the following prepositional phrase identifying the target of the whirlwind.
segments.s738-g43: 'چو' = 'when' (temporal); 'بشنید' is past 'he/she heard'; 'شیرین' is a proper name (the listener); 'که' is the complementizer introducing the content heard; 'آمد سپاه' is the embedded clause 'an army came' with 'آمد' (past 'came') and 'سپاه' (army) as subject.
segments.s738-g44: 'به پیش سپاه' functions as 'in front of / against the army' (به + پیش = 'to the front of' / 'in the face of'); 'سپاه' is 'army' and is possessed here by 'آن جهاندار شاه' = 'that world‑holding king' (جهاندار = sovereign/world‑ruler, شاه = king). The sequence names whose hosts are being opposed; Persian often places the locative/phrase 'به پیش سپاه' before the possessor for poetic effect.
segments.s738-g45: یکی is an indefinite subject 'one' or 'a certain man.' The noun phrase 'زرد پیراهن' places the adjective 'زرد' before the noun 'پیراهن' ('yellow tunic'), while 'مشک بوی' is a compound adjectival phrase 'musk‑scented' (مشک 'musk' + بوی 'smell') modifying the tunic; classical Persian commonly strings such modifiers after or around the noun.
segments.s738-g46: بپوشید is a past narrative form 'he put on / donned' (the b‑prefix is a classical/poetic variant here), followed by و 'and'. گلنارگون is an adjectival predicate 'pomegranate‑coloured / rosy' and کرد is the verb 'made' used causatively ('he made [his] face ...'). روی is the object 'face'; together گلنارگون کرد روی = 'he made (his) face pomegranate‑red.'
segments.s738-g47: یکی again = 'one (of them).' از here is partitive/attributive 'of/from' introducing the descriptive material; برش 'a cut / piece' (i.e., a cut or piece of cloth) combines with سرخ 'red' and دیبای روم 'Roman brocade' to describe the garment: 'one (in) a red-cut Roman brocade.'
segments.s738-g48: همه پیکرش is a genitive/possessive phrase 'his whole body' (پیکرش = پیکر + ـش 'his'). گوهر و زر lists the adornments 'gems and gold.' بوم here closes the clause; in context it functions as the locative/visual background ('on / about the cloth/body') or a copular/nominal closing in poetry meaning roughly 'was/appeared as' — the line thus means 'his whole body was gems and gold' (i.e., covered with them).
segments.s738-g17: This is a noun phrase with two coordinated nouns followed by a past participle used adjectivally. پلنگان و شیران (leopards and lions) are the head nouns and آموخته is a past participle functioning as an adjective ('tamed' / 'trained') placed after the nouns, as is normal in Persian.
segments.s738-g18: This is an instrumental/agent phrase plus a participial predicate. به here functions instrumentally ('with' / 'by'), زنجیر زرین is the noun + adjective 'golden chain(s)', and دهن دوخته is a past participle phrase ('mouth sewn') used predicatively — i.e. 'their mouths were sewn (shut)'. Word order places the instrument before the participle, typical of poetic/classical Persian.
segments.s738-g19: This clause gives an existential/count: قلاده بزر بسته is a noun phrase 'large collar(s) fastened', followed by the numeral صد and the copula بود, then سگ 'dogs' — yielding 'there were a hundred dogs with large collars fastened.' The participle بسته ('fastened / bound') modifies قلاده and the past copula بود places the whole phrase in the past.
segments.s738-g20: که introduces a relative clause ('which / that / who'). دردشت is a fused locative 'in the plain', آهو is the object 'gazelle', and گرفتی is a past active verb form ('seized / took') with an archaic ending; the clause modifies سگ. بتگ is dialectal/uncertain and appears at the end (left untranslated here).
segments.s738-g21: پس 'then' introduces the line; اندر ز رامشگران is a prepositional/partitive phrase 'among the ramashgarān' (here: tamers/trainers), with ز (poetic 'of/from') marking the group. دوهزار is the numeral 'two thousand' counting those within that group. The clause sets up a counted group which the next segment describes as acting.
segments.s738-g22: همه 'all' + ساخته (past participle 'made/arranged') form a predicative phrase 'all made ready'. رود here is the main verbal element meaning 'went / went forth' (an archaic/present-subjunctive form of رفتن used in epic diction), and روز شکار is a temporal phrase 'on the day of the hunt'. Note: the supplied sense list for رود gives only non-verb senses, so the correct verbal sense is marked as unavailable.
segments.s738-g23: This is a locative existential phrase. به زیر اندرون 'beneath the inner part/inner quarters' gives the location, followed by هریکی اشتری 'each one (had) a pack-animal' — هریکی quantifies, and اشتری names the animal. The prepositional phrase precedes the noun phrase as is common in poetic diction.
segments.s738-g24: This is a descriptive clause attached to the animal: به سر 'on (its) head' + برنهاده (past participle 'placed') indicates that something was placed on the head, and ز زر افسری specifies the material/object 'of gold, a diadem/helmet'. ز is the poetic genitive/partitive 'of/from' and افسری means 'diadem/helmet'. The whole phrase reads 'with a golden helmet placed on its head.'
segments.s738-g49: The clause is an inverted, poetic word order. به سر بر = 'upon the head' (به + noun + postposition بر), نهاد is the past of نهادن 'placed/put' (3rd singular), and افسر خسروی is the noun phrase 'royal helmet' with the adjective/possessive following the noun. Together they mean 'he set the royal helmet on his head.'
segments.s738-g50: نگارش is a noun meaning 'inscription/writing'; همه پیکر is a scopative phrase 'over the whole surface/body' (همه = entire, پیکر = body/surface), and پهلوی qualifies the script/language (Pahlavi). The phrase reads 'inscribed all over in Pahlavi.'
segments.s738-g51: از marks the point of departure ('from'), ایوان خسرو is 'Khosrow's iwan/porch', and برآمد is the past 'went up/rose'. ببام is a poetic fused form of بهِ بام 'to the roof' (directional complement) — here functioning like 'onto the roof.'
segments.s738-g52: به‌روز (here 'in the time/day of') + جوانی ('youth') forms a temporal phrase 'in the prime of youth'; نبد is a negative past copula 'was not / did not exist', and شادکام is an adjective 'fortunate/joyful'. The clause therefore says he 'was not fortunate in the prime of his youth.'
segments.s738-g53: همی‌بود is a past-continuous/habitual form 'kept on / continued to be'; تا introduces the temporal endpoint 'until' (here attached to خسرو, 'until Khosrow'), آنجا = 'there', and رسید is the past 'arrived'. The clause means 'he continued until Khosrow arrived there.'
segments.s738-g54: سرشکش (سرشک + enclitic) denotes a teardrop (poetic), ز = 'from', مژگان = 'the eyelashes', به رخ = 'onto the face/cheek' (به + رخ), and برچکید is a past verb 'fell/dripped' (burst forth). Literal: 'a teardrop from the eyelashes fell onto the cheek.'
segments.s738-g55: چو = 'when' (temporal conjunction), روی ورا = 'her face' (روی 'face' with ورا 'to her/there'), دید = past 'saw', and برپای خاست = idiom 'rose to his feet/stood up' (برپای = 'on his feet', خاست = 'arose').
segments.s738-g56: به پرویز is an indirect object 'to Parviz', بنمود is past of نمودن 'showed/made/displayed' (here 'made a display / offered'), and بالای راست is a noun phrase meaning roughly 'the upright/right posture' or 'the straight/right side'—poetically 'a straight/upright salute' directed to Parviz. The verb takes that phrase as what was shown/offered.
segments.s738-g89: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; مشکوی is an adjective in the genitive/attributive form meaning 'musk‑scented' modifying the following name; شیرین here is used as a proper name (the famous Shirin). بیامد is the past tense, 3rd person singular, 'came'. برش is a short, poetic/clitic element attached here (literally 'on him' or 'toward him') — a vernacular/poetic locative/oblique element linking the motion verb to its human target.
segments.s738-g90: ببوسید is a past tense verb 'kissed' (3rd singular) used with an implied subject from the previous clause. پای و زمین و برش lists three objects/places in series: 'the foot', 'the ground', and the final short/clitic element برش (here functioning like 'him' / 'upon him'); و is the simple coordinating conjunction 'and'. The verb precedes its objects in this poetic order but is a normal past form.
segments.s738-g91: به موبد is an indirect‑object prepositional phrase 'to the mobad' placed before the verb for emphasis. چنین 'thus' marks the following reported speech or manner. گفت is the past verb 'said' (3rd person); شاه 'the king' follows the verb (in Persian the subject can follow the verb). آن زمان places the utterance in time: 'at that time'.
segments.s738-g92: که introduces the content of what was said ('that'/'that which'). بر ما مبر is a negative imperative construction: بر + ما + مبر 'do not take/bring against us' (مَبْر is an archaic negative imperative of گرفتن/برداشتن here meaning 'do not entertain / hold'). جز = 'except' and به نیکی گمان = 'with a good thought/opinion' — the restriction: 'except with a good opinion'. Word order is the standard Farsi neg‑imperative plus the prepositional phrase of exclusion.
segments.s738-g93: مرین here is a contracted/poetic fronting meaning roughly 'this (for me)' (a fused demonstrative/benefactive — read as 'this' introduced before the object). خوب رخ را is the definite object marker construction: 'the beautiful‑faced one' (خوب رخ, with را marking it as definite object). به خسرو indicates the indirect object 'to Khosrow'. دهید is the present/subjunctive or polite imperative 'give' (here an order/request addressed to attendants).
segments.s738-g94: جهان را is the direct object 'the world' marked by را. بدین here functions as 'with this' / 'by this' (poetic fusion of ب + دین = 'with/by this'), linking to مژده نو 'the new glad‑tidings' which follows. دهید again is the imperative 'give' — the clause literally: 'Give the world by this new glad‑tidings.' The adjective نو modifies مژده ('new good news').
segments.s738-g95: مر is an archaic/poetic clitic used here as a beneficiary/dative ('for him' / 'for me' in older usage) attached before the object. او را is the direct object 'her' (او + را). به آیین پیشی is a prepositional phrase 'according to the rite/ceremony of precedence' (آیین 'rite; custom', پیشی 'precedence' i.e. being brought before); بخواست is past tense 'he desired / requested' (3rd singular). The whole clause means 'he requested that she be presented in the customary precedence.'
segments.s738-g96: که introduces an explanatory subordinate clause 'for/that'. آن is 'that' (pointing back to the aforementioned arrangement). رسم و آیین are coordinated nouns 'custom and ritual' (both feminine nouns meaning customary practice / rite). بد here is ambiguous/archaic in position but functions to comment on the state of those customs; آنگاه 'then' places it in time; راست is an adjective 'right / proper / fitting'. Together: 'for that custom and ritual were then right/appropriate.'
segments.s738-g1: چنان بد که is a result clause: چنان + adjective + که = "so ... that". The time phrase یک روز precedes the proper name plus title (پرویز شاه) in normal Persian word order: time → name → title; که introduces the following clause.
segments.s738-g2: همی is an archaic/prosodic marker used in classical Persian to indicate ongoing/habitual action; combined with the past tense کرد it gives a narrative past sense ("was longing / came to long"). آرزو is the noun object meaning "wish/desire" and کرد is the light verb "made/did" forming the compound "to wish". نخچیرگاه is the direct object ("hunting‑ground").
segments.s738-g3: بیاراست is a classical past/perfective verb (ب + آراست) meaning "he arranged/caused to be prepared." In older Persian narrative the following verb can appear in a short (undelled) form; برسان here functions as a past/actional verb meaning "sent/delivered" (older texts often show forms without a final -د). شاهنشهان is the indirect object/recipient "the emperors".
segments.s738-g4: که begins a relative clause modifying شاهنشهان: بودند is the past plural verb "they were" agreeing with the plural noun; از او پیشتر is a comparative temporal phrase "earlier than him / before him," and در جهان localizes it as "in the world." The clause literally: "who were before him in the world."
segments.s738-g5: چو is a classical conjunction meaning "when" (temporal). بالای سیصد is a numeral phrase meaning "over/above three hundred" (بالای + numeral). به introduces a prepositional phrase; زرین is an adjective "golden" modifying the following noun ستام. Together the phrase marks manner/instrument or a descriptive attribute of what was taken.
segments.s738-g6: ببردند is the past tense, 3rd person plural "they took / they carried." با usually marks accompaniment/association ("with"), and here introduces the person related to the action: خسرو نیک‌نام is the recipient or addressee (name + epithet). Note that classical narrative often omits an explicit direct object pronoun when context makes it clear.
segments.s738-g7: هزار و صد و شست is a classical way of listing numerals (thousand + and + hundred + and + sixty) — note شست is an archaic form for "sixty." The noun phrase خسرو پرست is a compound: خسرو (name) + پرست ("‑worshipper/‑devotee"), indicating people devoted to or followers of Khosrow.
segments.s738-g8: پیاده here functions adverbially/nominally to mean "on foot" (or "infantry"). همی‌رفت is the narrative past progressive "were going / marched." ژوپین بدست is a postpositive instrumental/secondary phrase: ژوپین (a padded jacket/gambeson) + بدست ("in the hand" / "carrying"), describing their equipment.
segments.s738-g57: زبان کرد is a light-verb construction: زبان (literally 'tongue' but here 'speech/utterance') is the noun and کرد (past of کردن) is the verb 'did/made' so together 'spoke' or 'uttered'. گویا before the following noun-phrase means 'as if / it seemed (that)'; بشیرین is the proper name Shirin in bound form (here marking the speech as 'Shirin's' or 'as if Shirin were speaking'); سخن ('speech') names what was uttered. Word order is noun + light-verb (زبان کرد), then a simile-marker (گویا) + the referenced person/name + the noun describing what was spoken.
segments.s738-g58: همی‌گفت is the habitual/past-continuous poetic form meaning 'kept saying' or 'was saying' (همی‑ is an older progressive/habitual marker). زان is a contraction-like prepositional phrase (به + آن) meaning 'from that / since that', and روزگار کهن is 'ancient time(s)' — together giving a temporal adjunct 'since those ancient days'. The segment supplies the time/duration for the speaking.
segments.s738-g59: بشست is the simple past 'he/she washed'. را is the definite-object marker showing that 'نرگس گل و ارغوان' is the direct object. The initial به here has a locative/directional sense ('to/toward' or 'on') often used in older Persian to mark the target of an action; the coordination گل و ارغوان links 'flower' and 'crimson/purple' as items washed together. Word order is (preposition) + list-of-objects + را + verb (object before verb, as is common in Persian).
segments.s738-g60: که at the start of this clause functions as 'because' and introduces the reason. بیمار بد is an intensified adjectival phrase ('sick / badly ill'), where بد modifies بیمار to mean 'badly ill'. نرگس وگل names the things that are ill ('the narcissus and the flower'), and درست here is an emphatic/adverbial particle meaning 'indeed/actually' or 'truly', completing the reason clause.
segments.s738-g61: بدان here is the fused prepositional expression به + آن meaning 'with/at that' or 'by that'. آبداری is a noun denoting the act of serving drinks/refreshments (an institutional/service noun). و آن نیکوی is a coordinated phrase 'and that good/excellent (one)' — both phrases form a circumstantial/associative prepositional phrase modifying the subsequent action. Note the older/fixed form 'بدان' instead of modern separate 'به آن'.
segments.s738-g62: زبان تیز is a noun + adjective grouping: زبان 'tongue / speech' modified by تیز 'sharp' (adjective follows the noun). بگشاد is a poetic past tense of 'to open' or 'to let loose' (here 'let loose / opened up'), used transitively of letting the tongue loose. برپهلوی is a locative adverbial 'beside / at the side (of someone)', indicating where the speech was directed. Overall this is 'he opened/let loose a sharp tongue beside (her/him)'.
segments.s738-g63: که introduces a relative/appositive clause 'who was ...'. The following items are a sequence of proper names and a title in apposition: تهما and هژبرا are names (classical personal names) and سپهبد is the military title 'spahbed' (here placed in the sequence as a title of the same person). تنا appears as a name-element or part of the name sequence. In classical Persian such lists of names/titles commonly appear without additional linking verbs; the relative pronoun provides the link back to the antecedent.
segments.s738-g64: This segment continues the sequence of epithets/names: خجسته is an adjective 'auspicious/blessed' qualifying the following title/name. کیا here functions as a title/name element (Kia), and گرد is part of a compound name or name-suffix (common in epic names like '-gerd'); together they form a personal name. شیراوژنا is an honorific/epithet meaning 'lion-like' or 'roaring' appended to the name. The whole line is nominal/appositive: adjective + name + epithet, completing the identification.
segments.s738-g73: به چشم اندر آورد زو خسرو آب = a single clause in which اندر آورد (simple past 'brought/put in') takes a direct object expressed by the prepositional phrase 'ز(و) خسرو آب' ('the king's water' / 'water from him') and the beneficiary/target 'به چشم' ('into the eye(s)'). Word order is verb-final in classical Persian: prepositional/locative phrase (به چشم) precedes the verb (اندر آورد) and the source/possessor phrase (زِ او/خسرو آب) follows it. The verb اندر آورد is 3rd‑person past and literal here: “brought/put into the eye.”
segments.s738-g74: به زردی رخش گشت چون آفتاب = predicative clause: گشت (past of شدن, 'became') is the main verb, preceded by a circumstantial phrase 'به زردی رخش' literally 'in the yellowness of Rakhsh' (i.e. with regard to Rakhsh's color). چون is a simile marker 'like/as', taking آفتاب as its complement. The order is typical: circumstance – subject (رخش implied) – verb (گشت) – simile (چون آفتاب).
segments.s738-g75: فرستاد بالای زرین ستام = فرستاد is the simple past 'he sent/dispatched' (3rd singular). بالای زرین ستام is a noun phrase serving as the direct object (an item sent): بالای here marks an object 'balāy' (an elevated ornament/canopy/insignia in classical usage) modified by the adjective زرین ('golden') and followed by ستام (a noun whose exact referent is literary/rare). The adjective زرین follows the head in this line of poetry, a common ordering in Ferdowsi's diction.
segments.s738-g76: ز رومی چهل خادم نیک‌نام = a prepositional phrase 'ز رومی' ('from Rûm') followed by a quantified noun phrase 'چهل خادم نیک‌نام' ('forty well‑reputed attendants'). Persian places the numeral before the counted noun (چهل خادم) and the epithet نیک‌نام follows as a compound adjective; the whole phrase names the people sent.
segments.s738-g77: که او را به مشکوی زرین برند = که introduces a subordinate clause ('so that/that/whose'). او را is the accusative 'him' (را marks the definite object). به + [adjatives] here ('به مشکوی زرین') functions adverbially/locatively — 'in/with a musk‑scented golden [item]' — where the actual noun (e.g. garment) is implied by context. برند is the 3rd‑person plural form (present/subjunctive or jussive in classical Persian) of بردن 'to carry/take', used here to express what the attendants will do: 'they carry/bring him.'
segments.s738-g78: سوی خانه گوهر آگین برند = سوی is the directional preposition 'toward'. خانه is the head noun 'house', modified by the compound adjective 'گوهر آگین' ('gem‑adorned' / 'jewel‑bright'), and برند repeats the verb 'they carry/bring' to complete the purpose/destination clause: 'bring toward the gem‑adorned house.' Repetition of the verb is a poetic device to balance the two infinitive/verb phrases.
segments.s738-g79: ازان جایگه شد به دشت شکار = ازان ('from that') + جایگه ('place/spot') mark the point of departure. شد (past of شدن) here functions idiomatically as 'set out / went' (literally 'became' but used in older Persian to mark change of state or movement). به دشت شکار is the destination phrase 'to the hunting plain'. The clause thus reads 'from that spot he went to the hunting plain.'
segments.s738-g80: ابا باده ورود و با میگسار = the line contains an older/poetic concatenation: ابا here should be read as a compact/colloquial sequence (effectively 'او با' = 'he with') or as an idiomatic phrase; grammatically the clause lists what accompanied him on entry: باده ('wine') and میگسار ('cupbearer'). ورود ('entrance/arrival') is a noun/verb indicating the act of entering; the coordinating و links the two accompanying items. The whole phrase says he entered with wine and with a cupbearer.

sentences.s738-s13: From the seat and the donkey-stall and the curtained chamber — that very tent and, at last, the pack-animals.
sentences.s738-s14: Five hundred camels stood before the inner quarters; they had all made that feast an engagement (a betrothal celebration).
sentences.s738-s15: Three hundred youthful horsemen of the kings rode along with the famous monarch.
sentences.s738-s16: With an aba, a yāreh, a necklace and a golden belt he set a jewel in a bead.
sentences.s738-s41: When he left the mountain and the plain, he went rejoicing toward the city.
sentences.s738-s42: They hung decorations in the city and along the road, for the king had come from the hunting plain.
sentences.s738-s43: From the sounding of trumpets and cries there arose a song; the air became voice, without warp or weft.
sentences.s738-s44: Thus that regal figure, lofty and with tall antlers, came from the plain into the lofty palace.
sentences.s738-s17: Two hundred slaves kindled the brazier; on it agarwood and amber were burning.
sentences.s738-s18: Two hundred young, obedient men — each with an aba, a narcissus and saffron.
sentences.s738-s19: They all carried (them) forward so that, when the wind bore the scent from every side, it might carry it to him.
sentences.s738-s20: They all went before that man who, sweetly scented, walked with musk in a hundred water‑vessels.
sentences.s738-s33: Where are all that love and blood-red teardrops — the sight of Shirin proved a poor physician for him?
sentences.s738-s34: Where are all those days turned into nights, the heart and eye weeping and the two lips smiling?
sentences.s738-s35: Where are all those bonds and our ties? Where are all those oaths and vows of ours?
sentences.s738-s36: He kept saying, and from his eyes yellow blood‑water flowed, dropping onto his lapis‑lazuli robe.
sentences.s738-s5: He carried a thousand and forty staves and swords, and he wore brocade beneath his armor.
sentences.s738-s6: After that he had five hundred wardens inside, also skilled in vashe, lamp-work and falconry.
sentences.s738-s7: After that three hundred horsemen rode off; then the wardens went with the cheetah-keepers.
sentences.s738-s8: Seventy lions and panthers were chained, tightly bound within folds of brocade.
sentences.s738-s21: When suddenly a whirlwind rose and fell upon that fortunate-born king.
sentences.s738-s22: When Shirin heard that an army had come to face the hosts of that world‑ruling king.
sentences.s738-s23: One donned a yellow, musk‑scented tunic and made his face pomegranate‑red.
sentences.s738-s24: Another wore a red-cut Roman brocade; his whole body was (covered) with jewels and gold.
sentences.s738-s9: The leopards and lions, tamed, had their mouths sewn shut with golden chains.
sentences.s738-s10: There were a hundred hounds with large collars fastened; dogs that in the plain seized gazelles — (بتگ).
sentences.s738-s11: Then among the tamers there were two thousand, all made ready and gone forth on the day of the hunt.
sentences.s738-s12: Beneath each inner chamber was a pack-animal, with a golden helmet set upon its head.
sentences.s738-s25: He placed the royal helmet upon his head; its entire surface was inscribed in Pahlavi.
sentences.s738-s26: He went up from Khosrow's iwan onto the roof; in the prime of youth he was not fortunate.
sentences.s738-s27: He continued until Khosrow arrived there; a teardrop fell from his eyelashes onto his cheek.
sentences.s738-s28: When he saw her face he sprang to his feet; he made a straight/upright salute to Parviz.
sentences.s738-s45: From the musk‑scented Shirin she came to him; she kissed his foot, the ground, and him.
sentences.s738-s46: At that time the king said thus to the mobad: 'Do not take (hold) anything against us except a good opinion.'
sentences.s738-s47: Give this fair‑faced one to Khosrow; give the world this new good news.
sentences.s738-s48: He asked that she be presented before him according to custom, for that custom and ritual were then proper.
sentences.s738-s1: It was so bad that one day King Parviz longed for a hunting‑ground.
sentences.s738-s2: He had it prepared and sent it to the emperors who had been before him in the world.
sentences.s738-s3: When they took above three hundred, with golden praise, to Khosrow the well‑named.
sentences.s738-s4: One thousand, one hundred, and sixty Khosrow‑devotees marched on foot, a jupin (padded coat) in hand.
sentences.s738-s29: He put forth words as if Shirin herself were speaking, and he kept saying them since those ancient days.
sentences.s738-s30: He washed the narcissus, the rose, and the crimson flowers, for the narcissus and the flower had grown ill.
sentences.s738-s31: With that serving of refreshments and that excellent one, he let loose a sharp tongue beside her.
sentences.s738-s32: — who was Tehma Hejbra, the spahbed Tana, the auspicious Kia‑Gerd Shiravazhna.
sentences.s738-s37: He put the king's water into (his) eyes; the yellowness of Rakhsh became like the sun.
sentences.s738-s38: He sent a golden balāy/ornament, and forty reputed attendants from Rûm.
sentences.s738-s39: so that they might carry him in a musk‑scented golden (garment) and bring him toward a gem‑adorned house.
sentences.s738-s40: From that place he went out to the hunting plain; he entered with wine and with a cupbearer.
